title: Focaccia, dimpled and oiled
subtitle: The Italian flat bread, generous with oil and sea salt
excerpt: A 30 × 20 cm focaccia, dimpled with oiled fingers and finished with sea salt flakes. Strong bread flour, a little yeast, a lot of olive oil. Mix on Thursday evening; bake on Friday.
sourceNotes: Focaccia is a Ligurian Italian flat bread with no single public-domain canonical source in British baking literature. The recipe here draws on the standard Italian regional tradition: strong bread flour (or 00 flour), high hydration around 75–80%, generous olive oil in the tin and on the surface, dimpling before the final proof. The fermentation method follows the slow overnight cold-proof approach (retard in fridge) used in northern Italian panifici; the dimpling technique is common to all regional forms. Baker's percentage anchors from the Ligurian tradition: hydration 78%, salt 2%, yeast 0.8%.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 focaccia (30 × 20 cm tin, cuts into 12 pieces)
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze pieces once fully cooled, wrapped tightly. Reheat from frozen at 180°C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix the dough the evening before; it sits in the fridge overnight. Transfer to an oiled tin an hour before you want to bake.

recipeTools.notes: Domestic ovens drift — worth checking for a bread that needs a hot oven.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique for developing gluten in a wet dough without kneading. Wet your hand, reach under one side of the dough, stretch it up, and fold it over the top. Rotate the bowl 90° and repeat. Four folds is one round.

content.text: Focaccia is the Italian flat bread that makes olive oil into a structural element rather than a finishing touch. The dough runs at 78% hydration, wetter than a tin loaf, and the oil goes in twice: once into the mix and again into the tin before the dough is pressed out. The result is a soft, open crumb with a crust crisp on the base and faintly blistered where the oil has worked into the dimples.

The overnight cold retard is the most convenient method for a home kitchen. Mix the dough in the evening, leave it in the fridge overnight, and transfer it to the tin an hour before you want to eat. The long cold ferment develops the flavour that a same-day focaccia lacks. Active work is under 30 minutes across two sessions; the fridge does the rest.

attrs.title: The oil quantity
attrs.body: The recipe uses 80 ml of olive oil in the dough and a further 3 tablespoons in the tin. This sounds excessive by bread standards and is correct. Focaccia is fried from the bottom in olive oil during baking. A thin smear produces a different result.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: 3 tablespoons, for the tin and dimpling

content.text: Strong bread flour gives the dough enough structure to trap the gas bubbles from a long cold ferment. Plain flour produces a more fragile crumb; 00 flour (higher protein for pizza) also works well.
Method
Mix the dough (evening)

Weigh {{strong-bread-flour}} strong bread flour into the large bowl. Add {{yeast-dried}} fast-action dried yeast and {{sea-salt-fine}} fine sea salt on opposite sides of the bowl (salt inhibits yeast on contact — keep them apart until the water goes in). Pour in {{water}} lukewarm water and {{olive-oil}} olive oil. Mix with a broad knife or your hand until no dry flour remains. The dough is very wet; this is correct.
Stretch and fold, then chill
Leave the dough to rest for 15 minutes, then do two rounds of
stretch and fold

with 15 minutes between rounds. Cover the bowl and put it in the fridge for 8 to 12 hours. The cold slows the fermentation to a crawl and develops the flavour slowly. The dough should roughly double in volume overnight; it may take longer in a cold fridge.
Transfer to the tin, 1 hour before baking

Pour 3 tablespoons of extra-virgin olive oil into a 30 × 20 cm tin and spread it across the base and up the sides. Tip the cold dough out of the bowl into the tin. With oiled hands, gently press it out to fill the tin. If the dough springs back and won't cover the corners, leave it for 10 minutes and try again; the gluten is tight from the cold and needs a short rest to relax. Cover loosely and leave at room temperature for about 1 hour, until the dough has puffed and feels airy when pressed lightly.
Dimple and finish

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Drizzle a little extra olive oil over the surface of the dough. Oil your fingertips generously and push them firmly into the dough to make deep dimples across the whole surface; the dimples should go almost all the way to the tin. The pressing action also redistributes the bubbles so the crumb bakes evenly. Scatter {{sea-salt-flakes}} sea salt flakes and the rosemary leaves over the surface if using.
Bake, 22–25 minutes

Bake for 22 to 25 minutes, until the surface is deep gold and the edges have pulled slightly from the sides of the tin. The base should be a dark golden brown; check by lifting one corner with a palette knife. If the surface is colouring but the base is pale, shift the tin to a lower shelf for the final 5 minutes.
Cool
Leave in the tin for 5 minutes, then slide onto a rack. Focaccia is best served warm, within two hours of baking. Once it cools fully, the crust softens.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: When things go sideways
attrs.intro: The three most common problems with focaccia.

items.symptom: Dough won't spread to fill the tin
items.cause: Gluten too tight, either from insufficient rest after the cold retard or from not enough stretch-and-fold rounds.
items.fix: Leave the dough in the tin for 10 extra minutes and try again. Do the full two rounds of stretch and fold the night before — it builds extensibility.

items.symptom: Flat, dense crumb with no open structure
items.cause: Dough over-proofed at room temperature before baking, or the yeast was old.
items.fix: After transferring to the tin, the dough should feel airy and puffed but not collapsed when pressed. If it deflates when pressed, it has gone too far. Also check the yeast expiry date.

items.cause: Not enough oil in the tin or oven temperature too low.
items.fix: The tin should have a visible slick of oil before the dough goes in. At 220°C fan, the base should be deep gold in 22 minutes. Domestic ovens drift — confirm with an oven thermometer.

content.text: Variations

A same-day focaccia skips the cold retard: after the two stretch-and-fold rounds, leave the dough at room temperature for 1 hour before transferring to the oiled tin for a further 45 minutes. The flavour is milder. A focaccia with olives presses halved pitted olives into the dimples before baking. For a thinner, crispier Roman-style version, use less dough in a larger tin and press it out to about 1 cm depth before dimpling.
